context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — SWOT Analysis

Recommended due date: August 15 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigation.

This assignment will be reviewed by course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with fellow learners for peer learning opportunities.

Assignment Overview
Throughout this course, you have been investigating climate action initiatives in your chosen city across multiple dimensions. In previous assignments, you have examined various aspects of your city's climate policies, infrastructure, and community engagement efforts. This week, you will synthesize your findings by conducting a comprehensive SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats) Analysis that evaluates your city's climate action potential across all Urban Pathways.

Understanding SWOT Analysis
SWOT Analysis is a strategic planning tool that helps identify internal and external factors that can impact an organization's or, in this case, a city's ability to achieve its goals. For climate action planning, SWOT analysis provides a structured framework to assess:
• Strengths: Internal positive factors that give the city advantages in addressing climate change (existing infrastructure, policies, resources, capabilities)
• Weaknesses: Internal negative factors that hinder climate action (resource limitations, policy gaps, infrastructure deficits)
• Opportunities: External positive factors that the city could leverage for climate action (funding sources, partnerships, emerging technologies, policy changes)
• Threats: External negative factors that could impede progress (economic pressures, political changes, natural disasters, competing priorities)

Assignment Instructions
• Re-evaluate your Spider Diagram. Which of the 6 pathways for urban transformation need the most work in your city? Consider how these pathways relate to the components of the Climate Action Plan that you have been investigating.
• Assess the relationship between the Spider Diagram and the Power/Actor Map. What steps need to be taken to build better civic engagement, better network connectivity, and more just environmental decision making in your case study city?
• Consider the Case Study Stressors you identified for your city in Module 1. Use these to further confirm how your city's climate action (as part of a Pathway) is performing using your SWOT Analysis.
• Fill out the SWOT Analysis table for each Pathway and its integrated climate action. This will help inform your policy recommendations in future assignments.

See SWOT Analysis Table Below

Completion Guidelines
For each cell in the table:
• Provide 2-3 specific, concrete examples
• Include relevant data, policies, or initiatives where possible
• Consider both current conditions and recent developments
• Think about how factors in one pathway might influence others
• Reference your previous assignments (Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, Case Study Stressors)

Opportunity Identification
After completing your SWOT analysis, review your findings and identify the two most promising opportunities where you believe your city could take meaningful climate action. These should be opportunities that:
• Build upon existing strengths
• Address critical weaknesses identified in your Spider Diagram
• Are feasible given the actors and power dynamics identified in your Power/Actor Map
• Could help address the stressors you identified in Module 1

Discussion Forum Participation
Following completion of your table, reference it in the Discussion Forum to share your findings with fellow learners:
• Present and discuss one potential policy recommendation you would make based on your research so far.
• Why is this recommendation important for your city? Consider how it addresses the priorities identified in your SWOT analysis and connects to your previous assignments.

Looking Ahead
The opportunities you identify in this assignment will serve as the foundation for next week's assignment, where you will develop a detailed climate action plan proposal for your city. Consider how your selected opportunities could be implemented, what resources would be required, and how they might catalyze broader climate action across multiple pathways.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk i'm kinda lost on the SWOT thing for my city rn. can u just explain the whole assignment from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—start with the big picture.

Your job is not to “solve climate change” for the city. Your job is to organize what you already found in earlier assignments into a SWOT table across the 6 urban pathways.

A simple way to think about it:
1. Look back at your Spider Diagram: which pathways are strongest, and which need the most work?
2. Look at your Power/Actor Map: who has influence, who is left out, and what affects civic engagement and fair decision-making?
3. Look at your Module 1 stressors: what climate pressures make action more urgent?
4. Then, for each pathway, sort your evidence into:
- Strengths = internal advantages
- Weaknesses = internal problems
- Opportunities = external openings the city could use
- Threats = external risks/barriers

For each box, you need 2–3 concrete examples, ideally with policies, data, or initiatives.

After the table, you pick the 2 best opportunities—the ones that are realistic, build on strengths, and help with weak pathways and stressors.

Let’s not stay vague. What is your case study city, and which 1 or 2 pathways in your Spider Diagram looked weakest?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep this brief and structured, since the student is lost at the start and needs orientation rather than a solution. To follow the tutor role and the assignment rules, I will not complete the SWOT or give examples for their city. Instead, I will clarify the task in plain language, break it into manageable steps, connect it to the prior course components named in the assignment, and end with a focused Socratic question that moves them toward beginning their own work.

exchanges.student: uh maybe heat + buildings? but also tbh i feel confused about strengths vs opportunities like they blur together for me.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now we have a real problem to work on.

The key distinction is usually this:
- Strengths = things the city already has or controls
- Opportunities = outside conditions the city could take advantage of

So ask yourself: is this already part of the city’s capacity, policy, infrastructure, or institutions? If yes, that leans strength. If it’s an external opening—new funding, state policy, technology, partnerships—that leans opportunity.

Test yourself: give me one item from your city related to heat or buildings, and I’ll ask you which side it belongs on.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified tentative weak pathways and a specific conceptual confusion. I should narrow the focus to the distinction between internal vs external factors in SWOT, using guided discovery rather than giving city-specific content. I will define the contrast minimally, then ask the student to test it on their own city with one example. This moves directly toward filling the table without providing answers.

exchanges.student: idk like maybe rebates or a cooling plan or smthn? can u give a few examples first bc i'm still not seeing it fr.
exchanges.tutor:
I’ll keep it generic so you do the city-specific work.

Think in pairs:
- A city’s existing cooling plan → more like a strength
- A new federal/state grant for cooling centers → more like an opportunity
- Existing building retrofit staff/programs → strength
- A utility partnership the city could newly use → opportunity

So: already in place vs available to leverage.

Now apply that. In your city, do you know of one thing that already exists for heat or buildings?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for examples, but I must avoid supplying content that could complete the assignment. I can still support with abstract contrastive examples that are generic and non-city-specific, then immediately return the cognitive work to the student. This uses guided discovery and remains bite-sized.

exchanges.student: maybe yeah but then i get stuck on like weaknesses vs threats too. it's all kinda blending together ngl.
exchanges.tutor:
Same split:
- Weaknesses = internal limitations the city has
- Threats = external pressures the city faces

Ask: is this a problem inside the city’s systems/capacity, or something outside making action harder?

Examples at the level of logic:
- weak building enforcement capacity → weakness
- rising extreme heat or energy prices → threat

Your turn: for your city, name one heat/buildings problem. Do you think it’s internal or external?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still conceptually confused, so I should clarify the second SWOT distinction in the same internal/external frame, keeping it concise and asking for application. I will not provide direct city-specific content or complete any table cells. This continues guided discovery and moves them toward independently categorizing factors.

exchanges.student: tbh can u just walk me through one full pathway from start to finish? i think seeing the whole thing would help more rn.
exchanges.tutor:
I won’t do a full pathway for you—that would cross into doing the assignment. But I can give you the exact process.

For one pathway:
1. Name the pathway and the climate issue in your city.
2. Pull 1–2 items from your Spider Diagram: why is this pathway weak or strong?
3. Pull actors from your Power/Actor Map: who can act, who blocks, who is excluded?
4. Pull stressors from Module 1: what climate pressure makes this urgent?
5. Sort evidence:
- Strengths: what already exists inside the city?
- Weaknesses: what is missing inside the city?
- Opportunities: what outside opening could help?
- Threats: what outside pressure could make it worse?
6. Then ask: which opportunity is realistic given the actors and power dynamics?

Let’s do step 1 only. What exact pathway are you choosing first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full walkthrough, which risks me doing the assignment. To follow the tutor rules, I should refuse to complete a pathway and instead provide a process template they can apply themselves. I will keep it bite-sized, structured, and end with a question that forces them to generate content for their city.

exchanges.student: ok maybe buildings i guess, but i'm still lost on what actually counts as “inside the city” vs “outside.”
exchanges.tutor:
Then use a stricter test:

“Inside” = the city already has it, runs it, funds it, regulates it, or has built capacity for it.
“Outside” = the city does not control it directly, but it can benefit from it or be harmed by it.

Ask of each item: could city government reasonably change this on its own in the near term? If mostly yes, lean inside. If not, lean outside.

Try one: for buildings in your city, name one item you’re considering, and classify it using that test.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeating the same conceptual distinction, so I should simplify the rule further and use diagnostic questions rather than provide assignment content. This follows Socratic guidance, keeps the response brief, and addresses the redundancy by narrowing to one test. I will avoid giving examples for the student's city and instead offer a decision rule they can apply.

exchanges.student: i'm not sure tbh, can u maybe just list the kinds of things i should look for in each box for buildings?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—look for categories, not answers.

For buildings:
- Strengths: existing building policies, retrofit programs, efficiency standards, city staff capacity, public building upgrades, utility coordination
- Weaknesses: old building stock, weak enforcement, low retrofit rates, poor data, limited funding, renter/landlord split incentives
- Opportunities: outside grants, state/federal standards, new partnerships, new financing tools, emerging cooling/efficiency tech
- Threats: extreme heat, rising energy costs, political pushback, housing pressure, grid stress, economic downturns

Now do the actual thinking: give me one possible item for each box, even if you’re unsure.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student needs more structure, but I still must avoid supplying partial solutions. I can provide category prompts for each SWOT box within the buildings pathway without offering specific content or wording to use. This is minimal scaffolding that directly supports their independent evidence gathering. I will end by asking them to generate one item per box to prevent passivity.

exchanges.student: idk if it's right but like old buildings, grants, heat waves, and maybe some existing policy? i'm still not super sure tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a workable start. Now stop guessing and classify them.

Ask yourself for each one:
- old buildings: is that an internal condition of the city or an outside pressure?
- grants: does the city already possess them, or are they an external opening?
- heat waves: does the city control that?
- existing policy: is that already in place inside city governance?

Put each into a box yourself, then send me your 4-box attempt.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced tentative items, so I should validate the effort without confirming a finished answer, then push them to classify each item using the internal/external rule. To avoid doing the assignment, I will not assign the boxes for them. I will ask a concise, solution-directed set of questions that helps them reason it out.

exchanges.student: ugh i'm still mixed up lol, can u just tell me if those go in the right boxes?
exchanges.tutor:
We’ve stayed on the same distinction for a while. Don’t stall here—make a first draft and refine it.

Use this rule only:
- internal existing advantage → Strength
- internal limitation → Weakness
- external opening → Opportunity
- external pressure → Threat

Now commit: write your 4-box attempt for those four items. Even if it’s imperfect. I’ll critique your reasoning after you place them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for direct confirmation, but the rules prohibit giving direct answers or partial solutions. They have also circled on the same concept repeatedly, so I should point out the redundancy, refocus on the bigger picture, and require them to commit to a written attempt. I can support by giving a decision test, but I must not sort the items for them.

exchanges.student: ok i think: strength = existing policy, weakness = old buildings, opportunity = grants, threat = heat waves. is that even basically right?
exchanges.tutor:
Basically plausible, yes—if you can justify each one with city-specific evidence.

Now the real test:
- What policy, specifically?
- How do old buildings hinder climate action in your city?
- What grants or outside funding source do you mean?
- What evidence shows heat is a serious external pressure there?

Give me one concrete detail for each. That’s how you turn labels into a SWOT table.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now made a concrete attempt after repeated prompting. I should acknowledge the progress, avoid giving definitive correctness judgments that would amount to supplying answers, and instead evaluate the reasoning conditionally. To stay Socratic and bite-sized, I will point them to the justification test for each category and ask them to strengthen each item with specific evidence from their city. This advances the assignment without writing it for them.
